messages.content:
correct this run on sentence:

My father retired from the Marines last year he is writing a book about his experiences.
My father retired from the Marines last year, and he is now writing a book about his experiences.
Jason mowed the grass this morning tonight he will water the flowers and bushes.
Jason mowed the grass this morning; tonight, he will water the flowers and bushes.
Nico seemed to be lost, she was crying and looking for her mother.
Nico seemed to be lost; she was crying and looking for her mother.
Jojie inherited a large sum of money from her brother she can buy a new car now.
Jojie inherited a large sum of money from her brother, so she can buy a new car now.
Ash and Charles worked very hard on their project therefore they felt they deserved a high grade.
Ash and Charles worked very hard on their project; therefore, they felt they deserved a high grade.
I can never beat my little brother at video games, he has them all mastered.
I can never beat my little brother at video games because he has them all mastered.
Ace is flying to Orlando for spring break, he will be gone a week.
Ace is flying to Orlando for spring break and will be gone for a week.
We are moving to Cincinnati in June I'll go to school there.
We are moving to Cincinnati in June, and I'll go to school there.
The hurricane passed quickly through the area last night, and it caused a lot of damage.

The sentence you provided is already correct; it is not a run-on sentence. It includes two independent clauses properly connected with a conjunction and a comma:

"The hurricane passed quickly through the area last night" is the first independent clause, and "it caused a lot of damage" is the second independent clause. The two clauses are correctly linked with the conjunction "and." 

"The hurricane passed quickly through the area last night, and it caused a lot of damage."
We will be on vacation next week we will be unable to attend your party.
We will be on vacation next week, so we will be unable to attend your party.
We bought shoes, luggage, hats, and swimsuits at the mall fortunately, we have a large trunk in our car.
We bought shoes, luggage, hats, and swimsuits at the mall; fortunately, we have a large trunk in our car.
We bought shoes, luggage, hats, and swimsuits at the mall fortunately, we have a large trunk in our car.
We bought shoes, luggage, hats, and swimsuits at the mall; fortunately, we have a large trunk in our car.
I didn't think it would be necessary to take my umbrella; after all, the sun was shining when I left home.

The sentence you provided is correct and not a run-on sentence. It contains two clauses properly connected with a semicolon:

"I didn't think it would be necessary to take my umbrella" is the first independent clause, and "after all, the sun was shining when I left home" is the second clause providing a reason for the first. The two clauses are correctly linked together with a semicolon, indicating a close relationship between the two thoughts. 

Here is the sentence again for your reference:

I didn't think it would be necessary to take my umbrella; after all, the sun was shining when I left home.
Most of my study time is spent on trigonometry it's my toughest class.
Most of my study time is spent on trigonometry, as it's my toughest class.
My car ran out of gas therefore I was late for my appointment.
My car ran out of gas; therefore, I was late for my appointment.
A fifth-grade student from our school won the spelling competition; she spelled words I had never heard before.

The sentence you provided is already correct. It contains two independent clauses connected properly with a semicolon:

"A fifth-grade student from our school won the spelling competition" is the first independent clause, and "she spelled words I had never heard before" is the second independent clause that adds additional information. The semicolon is used appropriately to link these closely related ideas.

Here it is again:

A fifth-grade student from our school won the spelling competition; she spelled words I had never heard before.
My younger sister collects aluminum cans to recycle and is saving the money for a new computer.

The sentence you provided is correct. It does not contain a run-on sentence or grammatical errors. Here it is again for reference:

My younger sister collects aluminum cans to recycle and is saving the money for a new computer.
Jason's dad is a pilot for a large airline sometimes he is gone for several days at a time.
Jason's dad is a pilot for a large airline; sometimes, he is gone for several days at a time.
My family reunion is in three months I wonder if everyone will be there.
My family reunion is in three months, and I wonder if everyone will be there.
I've missed several classes because I was ill I hope I will pass Philosophy class.
I've missed several classes because I was ill; I hope I will pass Philosophy class.
Jian is planning on going to Las Vegas for vacation; he'll need to take a lot of money.

The sentence you provided is already correctly punctuated with a semicolon to connect two related independent clauses:

"Jian is planning on going to Las Vegas for vacation" and "he'll need to take a lot of money."

Here's the sentence again for your reference:

Jian is planning on going to Las Vegas for vacation; he'll need to take a lot of money.
